narration: Alright, let's start in session.py because everything else in this PR builds on what changes here. The SessionStore class is gaining two new methods and modifying a third. First, create() now takes a scope parameter that defaults to 'default', so existing callers won't break, and it writes a rotated_at timestamp alongside created_at. Then the two new methods: rotate() deletes every session for a given user and returns the count, and touch() bumps the rotated_at on a single token. The rotate() method is the security primitive — that's what callers will use to invalidate sessions when something has changed about the user. One thing worth noting: rotate() does a hard DELETE rather than marking sessions as revoked. That's a deliberate design choice — there's no audit trail of the rotation event in this table — so if compliance needs to know who got rotated and when, that signal lives only in the application log, not the database.

concerns.text: rotate() is a hard DELETE — no audit trail in the sessions table. Any post-hoc forensics has to rely on application logs being retained.
concerns.suggested_question: Was a soft-delete (revoked_at column) considered for rotate()? Asking because we lose any database-level audit of rotation events.

concerns.text: touch() updates rotated_at but isn't called anywhere in this PR. May be intended for a follow-up.
concerns.suggested_question: Is touch() used by something outside this PR, or scaffolding for a future change?

look_closer_for: Schema migration for the new scope and rotated_at columns — does this PR include the migration, or is it expected separately?
What happens to in-flight requests holding a now-deleted token — graceful 401, or stack trace?
